cards.back: Layer-2 loops in switched topologies with redundant links. Without STP, a broadcast or unknown unicast loops forever, MAC tables churn, and the network melts down (broadcast storm). STP blocks redundant paths, leaving a loop-free tree, and re-enables them on failure.

cards.front: What 3 problems does an L2 loop cause?
cards.back: (1) Broadcast storms — broadcasts circulate forever, saturating links. (2) MAC address table instability — switches see the same MAC on multiple ports, constantly reflashing. (3) Multiple frame copies — receivers get duplicates, breaking applications.

cards.front: What is the bridge ID and how is the root bridge elected?
cards.back: Bridge ID = priority (4096 increments × extended system ID with VLAN) + MAC address. Switches exchange BPDUs; the lowest BID wins root election. Default priority 32768, modified to 32768+VLAN_ID by extended system ID. To force a switch to be root: lower its priority (`spanning-tree vlan X priority 4096`).

cards.front: Name the 3 STP port roles and what each does in steady state.
cards.back: Root port (RP): one per non-root switch — best path back to the root. Designated port (DP): one per segment (link) — forwards traffic on that segment; root has all DPs. Blocking/Alternate port: any non-root, non-designated port — receives BPDUs but doesn't forward data, prevents loops.

cards.front: Name the 5 classic STP port states (802.1D) in transition order.
cards.back: Disabled (admin down) → Blocking (only listens to BPDUs) → Listening (15s) → Learning (15s) → Forwarding. Total convergence 30-50s. RSTP collapses to: Discarding, Learning, Forwarding.

cards.front: How is the root port chosen when multiple paths exist?
cards.back: Tiebreakers in order: (1) Lowest cumulative path cost to root. (2) Lowest bridge ID of advertising neighbor. (3) Lowest sender port ID. (4) Lowest local port priority/ID. Cost is per-link based on speed (10 Gbps=2, 1 Gbps=4, 100 Mbps=19, 10 Mbps=100 — long path cost differs).

cards.front: What's the difference between PVST+ and standard 802.1D?
cards.back: Cisco PVST+ runs an independent STP instance per VLAN, allowing per-VLAN root and load-sharing across redundant trunks. Default on Cisco switches. 802.1D is single-instance (one STP for the whole bridged network); MST groups multiple VLANs into one instance.

cards.front: What happens to convergence time when a link fails in classic 802.1D STP?
cards.back: Up to 50 seconds: 20s max-age + 15s listening + 15s learning. Way too slow for modern networks — which is why RSTP (sub-second convergence with proposal/agreement and edge ports) is standard now.

cards.front: What does `show spanning-tree` (or `show spanning-tree vlan X`) reveal?
cards.back: Per-VLAN: root bridge ID + cost + port, this switch's bridge ID, hello/forward-delay/max-age timers, then per-port: role (Root/Desg/Altn), state (FWD/BLK/DIS), cost, priority/portID. The first thing to check when diagnosing root election issues.

cards.front: Memorize 802.1D STP path costs by interface speed: 10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps.
cards.back: 10 Mbps = 100. 100 Mbps = 19. 1 Gbps = 4. 10 Gbps = 2. (Newer 'long' / IEEE 802.1D-2004 cost values: 10M=2,000,000; 100M=200,000; 1G=20,000; 10G=2,000; 100G=200. Cisco still defaults to the short values unless `spanning-tree pathcost method long` is configured.)

cards.front: What does each classic STP port state DO? (Disabled, Blocking, Listening, Learning, Forwarding)
cards.back: Disabled: admin down, nothing. Blocking: receives BPDUs only — no send, no MAC learning, no data. Listening: sends + receives BPDUs, no MAC learning, no data (15s). Learning: sends + receives BPDUs, learns MACs, no data (15s). Forwarding: full operation. A Forwarding port CAN move directly to Blocking (link loss); a Blocking port CANNOT move directly to Forwarding (must go through Listening/Learning).

cards.front: Default STP timers (802.1D): Hello, Forward Delay, Max Age?
cards.back: Hello = 2 seconds. Forward Delay = 15 seconds (per state, so listening 15 + learning 15 = 30). Max Age = 20 seconds (= 10 × hello). Total convergence after a topology change ≈ 50 seconds (20 + 15 + 15). Timers configured on the root bridge are advertised to all switches.

cards.front: What MAC addresses do standard STP and PVST+ BPDUs use as destination?
cards.back: Standard 802.1D STP: 0180.C200.0000 (IEEE multicast). PVST+: 0100.0CCC.CCCD (Cisco multicast). PVST+ also adds an SSTP shim header so per-VLAN BPDUs can traverse trunks correctly.

cards.front: What does PortFast do, and where is it safe to enable?
cards.back: Lets a port skip the listening/learning states and move directly to Forwarding when it comes up. Enables on access ports connected to end hosts (PCs, printers) — not switch-to-switch links. Per-port: `spanning-tree portfast`. Globally on all access ports: `spanning-tree portfast default`. If a PortFast port receives a BPDU, it loses PortFast status (or, with BPDU Guard, is shut down).

cards.front: What do BPDU Guard, Root Guard, and Loop Guard each do?
cards.back: BPDU Guard: if any BPDU is received on the port, it's err-disabled (shut). Used with PortFast to prevent rogue switch attachment. `spanning-tree bpduguard enable` per port, or `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default` globally. Root Guard: if a superior BPDU is received, port goes 'root-inconsistent' (blocked) — prevents a new switch from becoming root. Loop Guard: if a non-designated port STOPS receiving BPDUs, it goes 'loop-inconsistent' (blocked) — prevents a unidirectional link from forming a loop.

cards.front: How do you make a Cisco switch the root bridge for VLAN X (primary or secondary)?
cards.back: `spanning-tree vlan X root primary` → sets priority to 24576 (or 4096 less than current root, whichever is lower). `spanning-tree vlan X root secondary` → sets priority to 28672. Or set explicitly: `spanning-tree vlan X priority N` (N must be a multiple of 4096, range 0-61440).

cards.front: What are the Bridge Priority field changes when Extended System ID is in use?
cards.back: Original bridge priority = 16 bits (0-65535, default 32768). With Extended System ID, the priority is split: 4 bits of priority (configurable in 4096 increments: 0, 4096, 8192, ..., 61440) + 12 bits of VLAN ID (the extended system ID). So actual advertised priority = (configured priority) + (VLAN ID). Default 32768 + VLAN 1 = 32769.

cards.front: Configure the STP cost or port-priority of a specific interface for a VLAN?
cards.back: `spanning-tree vlan <id> cost <cost>` — overrides the speed-derived cost. `spanning-tree vlan <id> port-priority <prio>` — overrides default 128 (range 0-240, in increments of 16). Lower wins. Used to influence root port / designated port elections when bridge IDs and costs tie.
